hook: A serene stuccoed quadrangle of charity homes built by the Goldsmiths' Company in 1811 on land an Acton goldsmith left them in 1657.
facts: Grade II* listed; erected 1811 to designs by Charles Beazley under the Perryn Trust, originally 12 almshouses with 8 added in 1838.
The land came to the Worshipful Company of Goldsmiths in 1657 under the will of John Perryn, an alderman and goldsmith who lived in Acton.
Three two-storey ranges of brown brick set round an open court, closed on the fourth side by a cast-iron screen and railings.
The central feature carries a pediment with a clock in the tympanum, a semicircular Ionic porch, and two staffs of Mercury (the Goldsmiths' emblem).
w: A serene stuccoed quadrangle of charity homes, raised by the Goldsmiths' Company in 1811 on land an Acton goldsmith willed them back in 1657. Three brown-brick ranges close round a court behind an iron screen; over the porch sit a clock and two staffs of Mercury, the company's emblem. Twelve homes at first, eight more by 1838.

hook: Victorian South Acton was nicknamed 'Soapsuds Island' — the washhouse of London, where thousands of hand-launderers scrubbed the linen of West End hotels and the rich.
facts: Soft local water made Acton a laundry centre: about 60 laundries in 1873 rose to over 170 in South Acton by 1890, most washing done by hand.
The 1901 census recorded 568 men and 2,448 women in Acton working as laundry workers, with specialist roles such as ironer and 'dollyman'.
The laundries served hotels and wealthy households in London's West End, earning the nickname 'Soapsuds Island' (also 'Soap Sud City').
Numbers fell to about 50 automated laundries by 1956 — still Britain's largest concentration — and the last closed in the 1970s.
The crowded 19th-century streets were cleared after WWII for the South Acton estate, today the largest housing estate in west London.
w: Victorian South Acton went by a nickname: Soapsuds Island, the washhouse of London. Soft local water drew over 170 hand-laundries by 1890, and the 1901 census counted nearly 2,500 women scrubbing the linen of West End hotels. The last closed in the 1970s; the crowded streets were swept away for west London's largest estate.

hook: A romantic mock-Gothic 'abbey' that never was — a stagecoach magnate's 1806 fantasy mansion, later a Catholic nursing home, now a brooding derelict by the North Circular.
facts: In 1806 stagecoach proprietor Thomas Willan bought West Twyford's manor house and had architect William Atkinson remodel it into a Gothic mansion he called 'Twyford Abbey' — though it was never an abbey.
The Alexian Brothers, a Roman Catholic nursing order, bought it in 1902 and ran it as a nursing home, enlarging the building several times.
The mansion was listed (Grade II) in 1973; the nursing home closed in 1988 and it has stood derelict ever since.
Planning permission was granted around 2021 to restore the building as an independent school.
legend: Willan deliberately gave the house a pseudo-monastic, faux-ecclesiastical air, fostering the legend of a medieval 'abbey' on a site that had only ever held a manor house and a tiny parish church.
w: A romantic mock-Gothic abbey that never was -- a stagecoach magnate's 1806 fantasy, dressed up by William Atkinson with all the faux-monastic trimmings on a site that only ever held a manor house. The Alexian Brothers ran it as a nursing home until 1988; it has brooded derelict by the North Circular ever since. Pure invented antiquity.

hook: A working store opposite Acton Town station holding the iceberg beneath the Covent Garden museum: hundreds of thousands of objects, from vintage Tube stock to enamel signs.
facts: The Museum Depot is at 2 Museum Way, 118-120 Gunnersbury Lane, Acton, W3 9BQ, directly opposite Acton Town Underground station.
It holds the majority of the London Transport Museum's collection not on display in Covent Garden — well over 300,000 objects.
Opened on its rail-connected site on 14 October 1999, it was the first museum store in the UK specifically designed for regular public access.
It is a store and restoration centre open only for guided tours and periodic Open Days, not as a day-to-day museum.
w: The iceberg beneath the Covent Garden museum, parked opposite Acton Town: a working store and restoration shed holding over 300,000 objects, from vintage Tube stock to enamel signage. Opened in 1999, the first museum store in Britain built for public access -- though only on guided tours and Open Days. Aladdin's cave on rails.

hook: Charles Holden's brick, concrete and glass interchange of 1932 — a textbook of his 'modern European' Underground style, with clerestory-lit ticket hall and concrete platform canopies.
facts: The station was rebuilt in 1931-32 by Charles Holden ahead of switching the Uxbridge branch from the District line to the Piccadilly line, which reached Acton Town on 4 July 1932.
Holden's design pairs a tall, block-like ticket hall (brick walls punctuated with clerestory glazing under a flat concrete slab roof) with a low horizontal range of offices and shops.
Platform canopies are carried on paired piers in alternating broad and narrow bays, an exemplar of Holden's mature interchange-station manner.
The station has been Grade II listed since 17 May 1994 as an important example of Holden's work.
w: Charles Holden's brick, concrete and glass interchange of 1932, a textbook of his cool modern-European Underground manner. A tall clerestory-lit ticket hall meets a low range of shops; paired piers carry the platform canopies in broad and narrow bays. Built when the Piccadilly line reached Acton Town. Listed, and quietly influential.

hook: On Acton Vale stood the works of D. Napier & Son — first luxury motor cars, then the legendary Napier Lion aero engine — anchoring Acton and Park Royal's car-and-aviation boom.
facts: Napier moved to a new plant at Acton in 1903 (works later addressed at 211 Acton Vale, W3) after car production outgrew its Lambeth factory.
The Acton works built luxury motor cars, then switched to aero engines, most famously designing and producing the 12-cylinder Napier Lion from 1916.
During WWI Napier was contracted to build 600 aircraft at Acton (R.E.7, R.E.8 and Sopwith Snipes) alongside engines.
Napier's success helped seed the Park Royal industrial cluster in north Acton — Wilkinson Sword, CAV and Lucas among them — on land freed from London County Council building restrictions.
Napier Aero Engines Ltd (with Rolls-Royce) took over the aero business in 1961 and the Acton engine works closed about two years later.
w: On Acton Vale stood the works of D. Napier and Son -- luxury motor cars first, then the mighty 12-cylinder Napier Lion aero engine from 1916, with 600 aircraft built here through the Great War. The plant seeded the whole Park Royal cluster: CAV, Lucas, Wilkinson Sword. The engine works closed around 1963; the boom it lit outlived it.

n: Acton County Grammar School site (The Who)
hook: On Gunnersbury Lane stood the grammar school where three Acton boys — Roger Daltrey, John Entwistle and Pete Townshend — met and set The Who in motion.
facts: Acton County Grammar School opened in 1906 on Gunnersbury Lane, just north of Acton Town station; future Who members Pete Townshend and John Entwistle were pupils, with Roger Daltrey in the year above.
Daltrey founded the group that became The Who while at the school in 1961, recruiting Entwistle, who in turn proposed bringing in Townshend.
Townshend and Entwistle became friends in their second year there and first played together in a trad-jazz group.
The old buildings were demolished in 2005 and rebuilt on the same Gunnersbury Lane site (the school later became Ark Acton Academy).
w: On Gunnersbury Lane stood the grammar school where three Acton boys collided: Roger Daltrey, a year above, pulled in John Entwistle, who proposed a guitarist named Pete Townshend. From that came the band that turned into The Who. The old buildings fell in 2005, rebuilt on the same ground. Maximum R and B started in a school corridor.

hook: A Georgian riverside terrace house that the Emery Walker Trust calls the last authentic Arts & Crafts interior in Britain — Morris wallpapers, Philip Webb furniture, frozen since 1933.
facts: No. 7 Hammersmith Terrace is one of seventeen terraced houses built c. 1755, and is Grade II* listed.
Engraver and printer Emery Walker, a close friend of William Morris and a key Arts & Crafts figure, moved into No. 7 in 1903 after living at No. 3 for 25 years.
The interior survives with hand-printed Morris & Co. wallpapers, furniture designed by Philip Webb, and Middle Eastern rugs and ceramics.

The Emery Walker Trust, which owns the house, describes it as the last authentic Arts & Crafts interior in Britain; the survival is owed to daughter Dorothy Walker preserving it after Emery's death in 1933.
The house is open to the public by guided tour.
w: A Georgian riverside terrace the Emery Walker Trust calls the last authentic Arts and Crafts interior in Britain -- Morris wallpapers, Philip Webb furniture, Persian rugs, frozen since 1933. The engraver and Morris confidant moved into number 7 in 1903; his daughter Dorothy kept every room as he left it. Time-capsule by the Thames.

hook: A former Victorian iron foundry turned film and TV studio where William Hartnell's Doctor Who regenerated and Tony Hancock recorded his half hour — rebuilt and reopened on its riverside site in 2019.
facts: A former Victorian iron foundry on Crisp Road was bought by Triumph Films in 1933 and converted into a film studio.
The BBC acquired the studios in 1954 for its television service and used them until 1974.
Series 2 to 6 of Hancock's Half Hour (1957-1960) were made there.
Episodes of Doctor Who were made at Riverside between 1964 and 1968; Studio 1 was where First Doctor William Hartnell's regeneration scene was filmed.
After becoming an arts centre in 1976, the building closed for redevelopment in September 2014 and a new Riverside Studios reopened on its original site in August 2019.
w: A Victorian iron foundry turned film and television studio, where Tony Hancock recorded his half hour and William Hartnell's Doctor Who first regenerated. Triumph Films converted it in 1933; the BBC filmed here from 1954 to 1974. After decades as an arts centre it was demolished and rebuilt on the same riverside site, reopening in 2019.

hook: Walk in through a plain 1970s shopping precinct and climb two floors into Frank Matcham's 1895 Victorian rococo auditorium — dismantled, saved, and rebuilt piece by piece inside the modern shell.
facts: The theatre was rebuilt and enlarged by theatrical architect Frank Matcham, opening in 1895 as the New Lyric Opera House with an address by Lillie Langtry.
Threatened with closure and demolition in 1966, it was saved by a public campaign.
The Matcham auditorium was dismantled and reinstalled piece by piece within a modern shell on King Street, a short distance from the original Bradmore Grove site.
The relocated theatre reopened in 1979 on Lyric Square, off King Street.
The new site was part of the former Hammersmith Palace Theatre, which had been demolished in 1950.
w: Walk in off a plain 1970s precinct, climb two floors, and step into Frank Matcham's 1895 rococo auditorium -- dismantled piece by piece when it faced the wreckers in 1966, then rebuilt inside the modern shell on King Street. Lillie Langtry opened the original; it reopened on Lyric Square in 1979. A Victorian jewel smuggled upstairs.

hook: The art-deco picture palace where David Bowie killed off Ziggy Stardust on stage in July 1973 — still hiding a working 1930s Compton organ behind the stalls.
facts: Designed by Robert Cromie in the Art Deco style and built 1930-1932, opening in 1932 as the Gaumont Palace cinema.
Renamed the Hammersmith Odeon in 1962; given Grade II listed status in 1990.
In July 1973 David Bowie performed his final concert as Ziggy Stardust here, filmed by documentary maker D. A. Pennebaker and later released as Ziggy Stardust and the Spiders from Mars.
The Beatles played 38 performances at the venue between 1964 and 1965.
The building retains a fully restored 1930s Compton organ.
w: The art-deco picture palace where David Bowie killed off Ziggy Stardust on stage in July 1973, with Pennebaker's cameras rolling. Robert Cromie built it as the Gaumont Palace in 1932; the Beatles played 38 nights here in 1964-65. Behind the stalls a fully restored 1930s Compton organ still waits. Grade II glamour on the Broadway.

hook: Marooned beside the Hammersmith flyover, this church preserves a bronze bust of the 'martyr' Charles I beneath which a Royalist merchant had his own heart buried in an urn.
facts: Hammersmith's first chapel here was built in the early 1660s by Sir Nicholas Crispe, who ran the local brickworks.
The church holds a wall monument to Crispe incorporating a bronze bust of King Charles I attributed to Hubert Le Sueur.

Crispe, an ardent Royalist, paid for the bust and requested that his heart be buried in a stone urn beneath it; he died in 1665 (buried at St Mildred Bread Street, his heart later reunited with his body).
The church was rebuilt 1882-1891 by the Chamberlen Brothers, and the Crispe monument and bust were transferred into the new building.
It lost graves and land when the Hammersmith Flyover was built in 1957-61.
legend: Tradition holds that Crispe so revered Charles I that he had his own heart interred in an urn under the king's bust, where it was annually refreshed with a glass of wine on the anniversary of the king's death.
w: Marooned beside the Hammersmith flyover, this church keeps a bronze bust of the 'martyr' Charles I, the gift of Royalist brickmaker Nicholas Crispe -- who asked that his own heart be sealed in an urn beneath it. The story goes the heart was refreshed yearly with a glass of wine on the anniversary of the king's death. Devotion, bottled.

hook: A white-washed riverside pub licensed in 1722, draped in rowing memorabilia, where Gustav Holst is said to have composed his Hammersmith Suite.
facts: The pub at 13 Lower Mall was first licensed on 9 June 1722 to John Savery, originally called the Blew Anchor and Washhouses.
It sits on the riverside walk beside Kensington Rowing Club, decked out with nautical and rowing memorabilia.
The pub appeared in the 1998 film Sliding Doors, with Gwyneth Paltrow's character dancing there after a boat race.
legend: It is claimed that Gustav Holst, who taught music nearby at Brook Green, composed his Hammersmith Suite at the pub, though there is no firm evidence.
w: A white-washed riverside pub licensed back in 1722 as the Blew Anchor and Washhouses, now draped in oars and rowing colours beside Kensington Rowing Club. Gwyneth Paltrow danced here in 'Sliding Doors'. Local lore has Gustav Holst, who taught nearby, composing his Hammersmith Suite at a table -- pleasant to believe, hard to prove.

hook: A quiet riverside lawn that hides a buried tidal inlet — Hammersmith Creek once ran right through here, crossed by the High Bridge, until it was filled in for this 1951 park.
facts: Furnival Gardens is a riverside park on Lower Mall in Hammersmith's pedestrianised riverside area.
The gardens were laid out for the Festival of Britain in 1951 on a site cleared of riverside wharves and slum housing.
Hammersmith Creek, a tidal inlet of the Thames navigable by barges, ran through the site and was culverted and filled in when the gardens were created.
The creek had been spanned by a footbridge known as the High Bridge.
The gardens take their name from Dr Frederick Furnivall, the philologist and founder of the local Hammersmith Sculling Club for Girls and Men.
w: A quiet riverside lawn laid out for the 1951 Festival of Britain -- and hiding a buried tidal creek. Hammersmith Creek once ran right through here, navigable by barges and crossed by the High Bridge, until it was culverted under the grass. Named for the philologist Frederick Furnivall, who founded a sculling club for local girls and men.

hook: An Edwardian dance hall behind a plain shopfront, its six Austrian crystal chandeliers surviving a century as soup kitchen, bingo hall and snooker club before becoming one of London's prettiest gig rooms.
facts: Built in 1904 at 310 Uxbridge Road as a dance hall, with six two-tier lead-crystal chandeliers.
Served as a soup kitchen during the Second World War.
Was a rehearsal space in the 1950s-60s and a snooker/social club in the 1980s-90s before restoration.
Restored and reopened as a live-music venue in 2001 by owners Charlie Raworth and Emma Hutchinson.
legend: Local lore holds that it was one of three identical halls built by a publisher as gifts to his three daughters, though this story is undocumented in the building's records.
w: Behind a plain Uxbridge Road shopfront hangs an Edwardian dance hall of 1904, lit by six tiers of Austrian crystal. The chandeliers outlasted a century of hard use -- wartime soup kitchen, snooker club, bingo -- before a 2001 restoration made it one of London's prettiest gig rooms.

hook: A celebrated new-writing theatre housed since 2011 in the ornate Passmore Edwards free library, a Victorian gift to Shepherd's Bush that fell empty when Westfield's library opened.
facts: The library was built 1895-96, designed by Maurice Bingham Adams in English Renaissance style, and funded by philanthropist John Passmore Edwards.
It was opened on 25 June 1896 by Archibald Primrose, 5th Earl of Rosebery.
The library closed in 2008 after a new one opened as part of the Westfield London development.
The Bush Theatre, founded in 1972 above a pub on Shepherd's Bush Green, reopened in the former library building in October 2011.
w: A celebrated new-writing theatre tucked since 2011 into an ornate Victorian free library -- Maurice Adams's 1896 Passmore Edwards gift to Shepherd's Bush, opened by Lord Rosebery. When Westfield's library replaced it, the old reading rooms fell empty; the Bush, born above a pub in 1972, moved in. Drama in the stacks.

hook: A pair of red-brick Edwardian engine houses, the oldest surviving power station built for the London Underground, later disguised on screen as a cartoon factory and the British Museum.
facts: Built in 1898, designed by Harry Bell Measures, to generate electricity for the Central London Railway (now the Central line).
The power station closed on 18 March 1928 once power came from Lots Road; the Italian tool firm Dimco later occupied the buildings, giving them their name.
Grade II listed since 1988 as the earliest surviving generating station built for the Underground.
Used as the Acme Factory in Who Framed Roger Rabbit (1988) and as the interior of the British Museum in The Mummy Returns (2001).
The buildings now front White City bus station beside Westfield London.
legend: Often labelled online as a 'Ghostbusters' location; the documented film credits are Who Framed Roger Rabbit and The Mummy Returns, not Ghostbusters.
w: A pair of red-brick Edwardian engine houses, the oldest power station ever built for the London Underground -- raised in 1898 to electrify the Central line, idle since 1928. On screen they have played the Acme Factory in Roger Rabbit and the British Museum in The Mummy Returns. Often mislabelled Ghostbusters; the credits say otherwise.

hook: The Brook Green school where Gustav Holst taught for nearly thirty years and wrote 'The Planets' in a purpose-built sound-proofed room that is still used for music lessons.
facts: Founded in 1904 by the Worshipful Company of Mercers from the endowment of John Colet, founder of the boys' St Paul's School.
Gustav Holst was director of music from 1905 until his death in 1934.
A sound-proof teaching room was built for Holst when a music wing was added; he composed most of his music there, including The Planets (c.1914-16).
He wrote his St Paul's Suite and Brook Green Suite (1933) for the school's pupils.
w: The Brook Green school where Gustav Holst taught for thirty years and wrote 'The Planets'. When a music wing went up, the school gave him a sound-proofed room of his own; he composed most of his work there, and dedicated two of his suites to the pupils. The room still hosts music lessons. The cosmos, scored on Brook Green.

a: Du Cane Road, East Acton, W12
hook: A twin-towered Victorian prison whose gatehouse carries plaster portraits of reformers Elizabeth Fry and John Howard, and over whose wall the spy George Blake vanished on a rope ladder in 1966.
facts: Designed by Sir Edmund Du Cane and built largely by convict labour, completed in 1891; Du Cane Road is named after him.
Two oval plaster reliefs on the gatehouse depict the penal reformers Elizabeth Fry and John Howard.
The gatehouse is Grade II* listed (a higher grade than the cell blocks, which are Grade II).
Soviet double agent George Blake escaped on 22 October 1966, five years into a 42-year sentence, helped over the wall by Sean Bourke.
legend: Popular accounts describe Blake's escape ladder as made from knitting needles; the rungs are usually said to have been size-13 knitting needles, a detail repeated in the press.
w: A twin-towered Victorian prison whose gatehouse carries plaster portraits of the reformers Elizabeth Fry and John Howard, watching over Du Cane's 1891 convict-built blocks. Over this wall the Soviet double agent George Blake vanished in 1966, five years into a 42-year term, up a ladder said to be stiffened with knitting needles.

hook: A vast Victorian glass barrel-vaulted exhibition hall, briefly England's largest enclosed space, that has held everything from the first World Scout Jamboree to Jimi Hendrix.
facts: Opened on 26 December 1886 as the National Agricultural Hall, designed by Henry E. Coe with the engineers Walmisley and Am Ende.
The Grand Hall sits under a largely glazed barrel-vaulted roof, with a floor of about 440 by 175 feet.
Its Grand Hall and 1885 Pillar Hall were Grade II* listed in 2003.
It hosted the first World Scout Jamboree in 1920 and a Jimi Hendrix Experience concert in 1967.
w: A vast glass barrel-vault thrown up in 1886 as the National Agricultural Hall, briefly the largest enclosed space in England -- 440 feet of floor under a single glazed roof. It has held the first World Scout Jamboree in 1920 and Jimi Hendrix in 1967, circuses to motor shows in between. Grade II* engineering, still drawing crowds.

hook: A long elm- and chestnut-lined strip of common land that once carried a polluted brook turned sewer, hosted a rowdy fair banned in 1823, and whose Queen's Head pub claims Dick Turpin as a regular.
facts: First recorded in a 1493 document, when a tributary of Stamford Brook known as Black Bull Ditch (or Parr's Ditch) crossed the marshy green.
The polluted watercourse was covered over and converted to a sewer by 1876.
An annual fair held from 1800 was banned after 1823 for becoming too rowdy.
The Holy Trinity Roman Catholic church was built on the south side of the green's eastern end in 1851-53.
The Queen's Head pub at 13 Brook Green occupies a pair of 1796 villas built for two brothers.
legend: The highwayman Dick Turpin is traditionally said to have been a regular at the Queen's Head pub on the green, a local claim with no documentary proof.
w: A long elm-lined strip of common where a brook once ran -- Black Bull Ditch, polluted into a covered sewer by 1876. An annual fair grew so rowdy it was banned after 1823. The Queen's Head pub, a pair of 1796 villas at the green's edge, still claims the highwayman Dick Turpin as a regular, on no evidence anyone can produce.
